coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of green emissive Plectranthus scutellarioides carbon dots for sustainable and label-free detection of phytohormone indole-3-acetic acid

coredata.dc:description: Plectranthus scutellarioides is commonly known as painted neetle or coleus, and widely employed in the Indian Ayurveda for several medical applications. In this work, green light emitting carbon dots (CDs) were fabricated using Plectranthus scutellarioides extract. The synthesized Plectranthus scutellarioides CDs (PS-CDs), possess green fluorescence properties, which was confirmed by UV-lamp at 365 nm. The fluorescent PS-CDs exhibit a pronounced emission at 530 nm when excited at a wavelength of 450 nm. As synthesized probe was stable for 30 days and was having the quantum yield of 21.7 %. The probe was used for the qualitative determination of indole-3-acetic acid (IAA) hormone. The probe depicts a good limit of detection of 0.0421 µM with a linear range of 0.05–2.5 µM for IAA sensing. The real sample analysis was performed in vegetable samples, showing good recoveries, which were well agreed with high performance liquid chromatographic technique analysis.

XPS analysis of PS-CDs (a) survey spectra, high resolution spectra of (b) N1s, (c) C1s, and (d) O1s.

The fluorescence emission spectra of PS-CDs at 450 nm were recorded after the addition of various phytohormones (6-BAP, IBA, strigolactones, zeatin, 2,4-D, kinetin, IAA, jasmonic acid and salicylic acid, 1 mM). (a) PS-CDs-impregnated paper strips for sensing of IAA at various concentrations ranging from (0.05–500 µM), (b) graphical representation of the intensity variation, by RGB colour intensity (weighted) values using ImageJ software (error bars depicting ± S.D.).
Schematic illustration of preparation of PS-CDs using P. scutellarioides leaves extract.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of green emissive Plectranthus scutellarioides carbon dots for sustainable and label-free detection of phytohormone indole-3-acetic acid

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Plectranthus scutellarioides is commonly known as painted neetle or coleus, and widely employed in the Indian Ayurveda for several medical applications. In this work, green light emitting carbon dots (CDs) were fabricated using Plectranthus scutellarioides extract. The synthesized Plectranthus scutellarioides CDs (PS-CDs), possess green fluorescence properties, which was confirmed by UV-lamp at 365 nm. The fluorescent PS-CDs exhibit a pronounced emission at 530 nm when excited at a wavelength of 450 nm. As synthesized probe was stable for 30 days and was having the quantum yield of 21.7 %. The probe was used for the qualitative determination of indole-3-acetic acid (IAA) hormone. The probe depicts a good limit of detection of 0.0421 µM with a linear range of 0.05–2.5 µM for IAA sensing. The real sample analysis was performed in vegetable samples, showing good recoveries, which were well agreed with high performance liquid chromatographic technique analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent times, there has been substantial effort to modify the properties of novel fluorescent nanomaterials. Carbon dots (CDs), an emerging category of intriguing nanomaterials with photoluminescent characteristics, have drawn a lot of interest in a number of optical sensing applications [1–3]. Less than 10 nm in size, these new nanomaterials have been used in electrocatalysis, chemical sensing, bioimaging, drug delivery, and optoelectronics, among other possible uses [4–7]. Numerous benefits of CDs include low toxicity, easy manufacturing, high water solubility, high quantum yield (QY), strong biocompatibility, and distinctive optical features [8–10]. The sustainable approach for the synthesis of CDs have piqued long term interest due to its biocompatibility and facile synthesis techniques. Various different types of green sources were used for the synthesis of CDs including lemon peel, Curcuma longa, Peltophorum pterocarpum flowers, medicinal plants like Andrographis paniculata [11–14]. In this study a similar method that is by using the plant leaf extract of Plectranthus scutellarioides (P. scutellarioides) was employed as a carbon source for the fabrication of CDs.

Combining ayurvedic knowledge with nanotechnology offers promising solutions to many current health and care challenges. P. scutellarioides is one such plant with significant medicinal properties. Native to southeast Asia, this species belongs to a family with hundreds of different coleus varieties. Due to their diverse leaf shapes and colours, only the red–purple leafed species is utilized in Indian ayurveda, while the others are primarily ornamental plants [15,16]. The extracts of P. scutellarioides can be obtained using various polar solvents, including butanol, hexane, ethyl acetate, ethanol, methanol, and water. According to available literature, the total flavonoid content in the various extracts of P. scutellarioides ranges from 1.75 mg/g to 16.78 mg/g when extracted using polar solvents [17–19]. Additionally, the extract contains other important chemical compounds such as 3-hydroxyspirocoleon 7-O-β-D-glucoside, spiroscutelones A-C, scutellarioidolide A, and sincoetsin C [20–22]. The extracts exhibit antioxidative properties, attributed to the presence of natural substances such as flavonoids, which function as natural antioxidants. P. scutellarioides is extensively utilized in various medicinal forms, including infusions, decoctions, or fresh preparations, to treat conditions such as ulcers, skin infections, abscesses, ear and eye inflammations, blains, and rashes. Different parts of the plant are employed for specific medical ailments [23,24]. To increase its efficacy, it has been used as a precursor for the synthesis of various nanoparticles which had resulted in to a more pronounced medicinal effect [25–27]. A small part of the metabolome, phytohormones are plant-produced regulators that are particularly important since they control germination, development, reproduction, and the plants defence mechanisms against stress [28,29].

Indole-3-acetic acid (IAA) is a crucial phytohormone and one of the most important natural auxins, playing a key role in promoting cell elongation and division, which in turn drives overall plant growth and development. Sensitive techniques for determining IAA in biological samples have recently been developed including immunoassay, gas chromatography-mass spectrometry, liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS), and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [30–33], respectively. However, due to complex data collection, processing systems, high cost, competent operator and need for sophisticated scientific instruments, their use is severely limited. A type of universal phytohormone produced by plants, IAA is crucial for the regulation and development of plant growth. To gain a deeper understanding of plant physiological processes, it is essential to develop analytical methods that can accurately quantify or qualitatively assess IAA. However, the detection of IAA is significantly challenged by its low concentration in plant tissues, compounded by internal interferences from amino acids, saccharides, and pigments, as well as external environmental factors such as oxygen, light, and heat [34–36].

In this study, CDs were synthesized from a sustainable source (Plectranthus scutellarioides leaves extract) using a hydrothermal method. The as-prepared PS-CDs exhibit green fluorescence at 365 nm under UV illumination and display an emission peak at 530 nm when excited at 450 nm. These PS-CDs demonstrate commendable stability and water solubility. The as-fabricated PS-CDs have proven to be an effective fluorescent probe for selective detection of IAA. The probe operates on a fluorescence turn-off mechanism, where the green fluorescence is quenched in the presence of IAA (Scheme 1 ). The probe demonstrates a consistent detection range between 0.05 and 2.5 µM, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.0421 µM for IAA. The novelty of this work lies in the unique synthesis conditions and the precursor material employed for the detection of IAA. Specifically, this study presents the first reported method for synthesizing green-emitting CDs utilizing P. scutellarioides leaf extract as the carbon source. Notably, the prepared CDs exhibit a high QY, a significant advantage, especially considering the use of a plant-based precursor and the absence of doping, which often enhances luminescent properties. The probe demonstrates excellent sensitivity and selectivity, even in the presence of various interfering substances in complex matrices. Furthermore, the application potential of the synthesized CDs is expanded by their successful performance in real sample analyses and integration into paper-based detection system for IAA assay, further demonstrating their practical utility.

Leaves of P. scutellarioides were gathered from the local region in Surat. Technical-grade plant hormones were procured from Sigma Aldrich, while all other chemicals used were of analytical grade. Milli-Q water was used for the experimental analysis. The UV–visible spectrophotometer from Ocean Optics, USA and fluorescence spectrophotometer by Cary Eclipse, Agilent Technologies, USA were used for assessing the optical properties of PS-CDs. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) Bruker, ALPHA II, Germany was used for identification of the functional groups present in the PS-CDs. Field emission transmission electron microscopy (FE-TEM) was employed to analyse the morphological structures of PS-CDs by utilizing an FE-TEM, JEM-F200, JEOL, Japan. Zeta potential measurements and dynamic light scattering (DLS) were conducted with a Horiba Scientific SZ-100 V.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of Plectranthus scutellarioides phytochemicals and synthesis of PS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Leaves of P. scutellarioides were collected locally and cleaned with both tap and Milli-Q water to remove contaminants before being dried in a hot air oven at 50 °C for 24 h. The dried leaves were then ground into a fine powder. Two grams of this powder were dissolved in Milli-Q water and left to infuse for 24 h to extract phytochemicals. The resulting solution was filtered through Whatman paper, yielding a dark orange extract. This extract was stored at 4 °C for future use. To synthesize the CDs, an equal volume of the plant extract was mixed with Milli-Q water and subjected to carbonization in a pressure steam autoclave at 150 °C for 3 h. The resulting brown solution was then centrifuged and dialyzed for 6 h to achieve uniform-sized PS-CDs. The final product, a light-yellow solution containing PS-CDs, was refrigerated at 4 °C for subsequent use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of IAA via PS-CDs as a fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: To explore fluorescence sensing performance of PS-CDs towards phytohormone, we systematically prepared a series of various phytohormones solutions (1.0 mM). The phytohormones such as 6-benzylaminopurine (6-BAP), indole-3-butyric acid (IBA), stringloactones, zeatin, 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-D), IAA, kinetin, jasmonic acid and salicylic acid were selected for this investigation. To assess the interaction between the PS-CDs and these phytohormones, 0.5 mL of each 1 mM hormone solution was introduced into a 4 mL vial containing 1 mL of the PS-CDs solution. The mixtures were subjected to thorough vortexing for 120 s to ensure uniform mixing and interaction between the PS-CDs and the hormones. Subsequent to vortexing, the fluorescence spectra of each mixture were recorded, specifically monitoring spectral changes at an excitation wavelength of 450 nm. The selectivity and sensing ability of PS-CDs were examined based on the spectral shifts or intensity changes, which is an indicative of chemical interplay between the PS-CDs and the phytohormones. The data obtained from these fluorescence measurements provides insights into the sensing performance of PS-CDs towards IAA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellulose paper strip-based detection of IAA using PS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: A square strip of nitrocellulose paper, precisely measuring 1 × 1 cm, was prepared for the experiment. This strip was carefully immersed in a 3 mL solution of PS-CDs for 15 min to ensure thorough saturation. Following the immersion, the strip was allowed to air dry for 30 min under ambient conditions to remove excess material and stabilize the attachment of the PS-CDs to the nitrocellulose matrix. Subsequently, the paper strip, now conjugated with the PS-CDs probe, was exposed to a range of IAA concentrations, specifically 0.05, 0.5, 5.0, 10, 100, and 500 µM. This exposure facilitated the adsorption interaction between the PS-CDs probe and IAA. After sufficient adsorption had occurred, the strip was again subjected to air drying for 30 min to ensure stability. The dried strip was then examined under a UV-lamp at a wavelength of 365 nm to observe any notable variations in the green fluorescence of the PS-CDs probe. The variations in fluorescence intensity, which correlate with the concentration of IAA, were visually assessed to determine the probe's sensitivity and response to different IAA concentrations.

The QY of the PS-CDs was determined using rhodamine 6G (Rd-6G) as a reference standard. To begin, the absorption spectra of both the PS-CDs and the Rd-6G standard solution were recorded to assess their absorbance characteristics. Additionally, the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra were obtained for both the standard and the PS-CDs.

Where, QY PS-CDs and QY Rd-6G are the QY of the PS-CDs and Rd-6G, respectively. IPS-CDs and IRd-6G are the integrated fluorescence intensities of the PS-CDs and Rd-6G, respectively. APS-CDs and ARd-6G represent the absorbance values of the PS-CDs and Rd-6G at the excitation wavelength. ηPS-CDs and ηRd-6G are the refractive indices of the solvents used for the PS-CDs and Rd-6G solutions, respectively. This equation accounts for differences in absorbance, fluorescence intensity, and refractive index between the sample and the reference, allowing for the accurate determination of the QY of PS-CDs. The QY of PS-CDs was found to be 21.72 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of IAA in vegetable samples
ce:section.ce:para: Applicability of the as-prepared PS-CDs was determined by spiking vegetable samples with IAA. The vegetable sample such as carrot, tomato and apple were used. The vegetables were thoroughly washed and cleaned using Milli-Q water. The carrot, apple, and tomato peel were spiked with different concentrations of IAA (0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5 µM). The spiked samples were kept overnight in dark. After 24 h treatment, the samples were extracted using methanol for carrot and ethanol:acetone (1:1) for tomato and apple. The sample was then filtered using Whatman filter paper. The resultant solution was used to analyze IAA in spiked samples by fluorescence spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of PS-CDs and optimization parameters for PS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: For the synthesis of PS-CDs, an equal volume of P. scutellarioides leaves extract and Milli-Q water was thoroughly mixed. This mixture was then subjected to a carbonization process under pressure in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel vessel, maintained at a temperature of 150 °C for 3 h. The resultant solution was centrifuged and dialyzed further, yielding a light yellow-coloured solution, which confirms the formation of uniform-sized PS-CDs. The 6 h dialysis of the PS-CDs crude solution yielded green fluorescent uniform size CDs which were further utilized for the sensing applications (Fig. S1a of Supporting Information). The synthesis conditions, particularly temperature and time, were found to be critical factors influencing the formation and emission properties of the PS-CDs. To optimize the temperature for maximum fluorescence emission, a range of 100 °C to 250 °C was investigated, revealing that the highest emission intensity was achieved at 150 °C, beyond which a continuous decline in intensity was observed, suggesting that 150 °C is the optimal temperature for the synthesis (Fig. S1b of Supporting Information). Similarly, the reaction time was optimized by varying the duration from 1 to 4 h. It was found that the maximum fluorescence intensity of the PS-CDs occurred after 3 h of carbonization. Extending the reaction time beyond this led to a decrease in emission intensity, indicating that 3 h is the optimal time for the synthesis process (Fig. S1c of Supporting Information). These optimizations ensure the production of PS-CDs with the highest possible fluorescence efficiency, essential for the intended application.

The as-synthesized PS-CDs were assessed using fluorescence and UV–visible spectrophotometry, as shown in Fig. S2 a-b of the Supporting Information. The UV–visible absorbance spectrum of the PS-CDs displays a broad peak at 330 nm, distinct from the sharp peak at 346 nm recorded for P. scutellarioides leaf extract. The observed decrease in absorbance intensity further suggests the successful formation of the PS-CDs. The comparative analysis of fluorescence spectral data also validates the successful fabrication of PS-CDs as there is a remarkable shift and variation in the fluorescence intensity (Fig. S2b of Supporting Information). To further investigate the maximum emission of the PS-CDs, their emission spectra were recorded by applying λEx in the range of 410 to 500 nm. The maximum emission peak consistently appeared at 530 nm when the excitation wavelength was set at 450 nm, as illustrated in Fig. S3 of the Supporting Information. The UV–visible absorption, excitation and emission spectra of PS-CDs were depicted in Fig. 1 . When excited at 450 nm, the PS-CDs exhibited an intense emission intensity at 530 nm. The maximum absorbance band at 330 nm was observed in the UV–visible spectrum PS-CDs, which is indicative of an n → π* transition. Visually, the PS-CDs appear light yellow in natural light, and they emit a green fluorescence colour under UV illumination at 365 nm wavelength, suggesting the surface of the PS-CDs is rich in oxygen, which likely contributes to their unique optical properties. Moreover, the stability of the as-synthesized PS-CDs was investigated over a 60-day period. The spectral data indicated that the PS-CDs remained stable for approximately 30 days, after which a noticeable decrease in intensity was observed, as shown in Fig. S4 of the Supporting Information. This decrease over time could be indicative of changes in the surface chemistry or aggregation of the CDs, which might influence their optical properties. The QY of the PS-CDs was calculated to be 21.72 %. It indicates that a significant portion of the absorbed energy is re-emitted as fluorescence, reflecting the photophysical efficiency of the PS-CDs. This value is crucial for evaluating the probable applications of PS-CDs. The high QY of the PS-CDs can be achieved due to the biochemical composition of the phytochemicals present in the extract used as a carbon source. The plant extract used in our study for the synthesis of PS-CDs contains high number of flavonoids and phenolic substances which after carbonization introduces hydroxyl, carbonyl and amine groups on the surface of the PS-CDs enhancing the surface passivation on the surface of CDs. This can minimize non-radiative recombination of the photo-excited electrons and improve the fluorescence efficiency of the probe. The synthesis method and optimization parameters also helps in enhancing the QY of the probe. Also there are various reported methods for QY as high as 33.15 % and 43 % [37,38].

To check the presence of various functional groups on the surface of PS-CDs, FT-IR analysis was performed and shown in Fig. S5 of Supporting Information. The P. scutellarioides extract and PS-CDs surface were rich in various functional groups, favoring to generate surface active functional groups for biosensing application. The broad peak at 3443 cm−1 corresponds to –OH vibrations, which was identified in both the extract and the PS-CDs; however, a significant difference in the intensity was observed. The presence of hydroxyl groups results in higher water solubility and stability of PS-CDs [39]. The peak at 1652 cm−1 was attributed to –C=O stretching in the extract, which exhibited a reduction in intensity and broadening in the PS-CDs spectrum [40]. The sharp peak at 1377 cm−1 is associated with –NH stretching in the extract, also became broader and less intense in the PS-CDs [41]. Peaks at 1261 and 1317 cm−1 were assigned to C-O-C stretching in both the extract and PS-CDs [42]. Notably, a highly conjugated C-O stretching vibration at 1055 cm−1 was noticed in the extract, however it was absent in the PS-CDs FT-IR spectrum [43]. This disappearance suggests that the phytochemicals from P. scutellarioides are completely involved in forming PS-CDs with surface active groups.

The hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential data are depicted in Fig. S6 of Supporting Information. These measurements provide insight into the colloidal properties and stability of PS-CDs. The average hydrodynamic diameter of the as-synthesized PS-CDs was measured to be 3.05 nm (Fig. S6a of the Supporting Information). The zeta potential of the PS-CDs was found to be −37.8 mV, which indicates high colloidal stability of PS-CDs (Fig. S6c of Supporting Information). A highly negative zeta potential suggests that the strong electrostatic repulsion between the particles, preventing aggregation and ensuring stability in suspension. Further, morphological analysis using FE-TEM, as depicted in Fig. 2 a, confirmed that the PS-CDs possess a uniform and spherical shape. The size of the PS-CDs was determined to be 1.6 ± 0.3 nm.

To investigate the elements present in PS-CDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out (Fig. 3 ). The survey spectrum of PS-CDs shows the elemental peaks at binding energies 286.86, 409.06, and 534.64 eV for C, N, and O elements (Fig. 3a) [44]. The peaks at 399.59, 403.17 and 406.92 eV are related with various binding energies of N1s, relating to C–N, N–H and C–N–C binding energies present in PS-CDs (Fig. 3b) [45–47]. The deconvoluted spectra of C1s represents four distinctive peaks at 285.03, 288.25, 293.07 and 295.78 eV in the peak fitting shows the existence of the C=C, C–O, C–OH and C=N groups (Fig. 3c) [48,49]. The PS-CDs spectral data of O 1s at 531.84 eV confirms existence of C=O group (Fig. 3d). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of PS-CDs is suggestive the crystalline structure. The XRD pattern shows several peaks at 2 theta values at 28.71°, 29.35°, 31.02°, 34.12°, 40.83°, 45.56°, and 56.82°, which correspond to specific crystallographic planes within the material (Fig. S7 of Supporting Information). The presence of these sharp and well-defined peaks indicates a notable degree of crystallinity in the prepared PS-CDs, which is a key factor influencing their electronic and optical properties. Additionally, the pattern may include broad, low-intensity regions, suggesting the presence of amorphous carbon alongside the crystalline phase, a common feature in carbon-based materials [50–53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of fluorescence-based detection of IAA using PS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the sensing application of the as-synthesised PS-CDs towards phytohormones, various phytohormones such as 6-BAP, IBA, strigolactones, zeatin, 2,4-D, kinetin, IAA, jasmonic acid and salicylic acid were taken and mixed with the PS-CDs. The change in the emission intensity was recorded as shown in the Fig. 4 . It was observed that the addition of phytohormone IAA shows a remarkable change in the intensity of PS-CDs when excited at 450 nm. The noticeable change indicates that the PS-CDs acted as an effective fluorescent probe for the detection of IAA. The characteristic emission peak of PS-CDs show around 60 % quenching of the fluorescence intensity in the presence of IAA. This experiment confirms that the synthesizedPS-CDs act as a selective probe for the sensing of IAA.

The pH is a crucial factor that influences the stability of the fluorescence probe and its sensing mechanism. To assess this, PS-CDs were examined for their response to varying pH levels using phosphate-buffered saline (PBS). The as-synthesized PS-CDs was subjected to solutions with pH values varying from 2 to 12. The graphical data indicated that pH had no significant impact on the probe, as evidenced by the absence of notable changes in fluorescence intensity across the pH range. Similar experiments were conducted in the presence of the phytohormone IAA. The spectral data revealed no significant variation in fluorescence quenching across different pH levels (2–12) when IAA was present (Fig. S8a-b of Supporting Information). These findings suggest that the fluorescence sensing performance of PS-CDs is not affected by pH variations, indicating its robustness and broad applicability.

The quenching mechanism of PS-CDs was studied using FE-TEM, DLS, zeta potential, FT-IR and life-time analysis. The fluorescence lifetime analysis revealed that the decay times for PS-CDs and in combination with IAA were 2.31 and 2.16 ns, respectively. This slight reduction in decay time upon the addition of IAA is indicative of static quenching. The decay time analysis is shown in Fig. S9 of Supporting Information. These interactions result in the formation of stable ground-state complexes between PS-CDs and IAA, which effectively reduces the number of free PS-CDs capable of fluorescing upon excitation, resulting in the reduction of fluorescence intensity without significantly altering the lifetime of the excited state. The small reduction in fluorescence lifetime, further supports the predominance of static quenching, as this slight change can be attributed to minor alterations in the microenvironment around the PS-CDs due to complex formation. Upon the addition of IAA, the hydrodynamic diameter and FE-TEM results show the major changes. The hydrodynamic diameter of PS-CDs was increased from 3.05 to 13.2 nm (Fig. S6b of Supporting Information). This significant increase in the size is attributed to the aggregation of PS-CDs in the presence of IAA, which suggests that IAA induces interactions among the CDs, leading to form PS-CDs-IAA aggregations. FE-TEM analysis also reveals an increase in the particle size of PS-CDs from 1.6 ± 0.3 to 7.1 ± 0.9 nm with addition of IAA (Fig. 2c–d). This FE-TEM results are well agreed with the DLS results, further supports the conclusion that IAA induces aggregation of the PS-CDs, altering their morphological, structural, optical and colloidal properties.

The FT-IR analysis of PS-CDs and IAA reveals significant non-covalent interactions that contribute to the stability of the PS-CDs + IAA complex (Fig. S10 of Supporting Information). Key interactions include hydrogen bonding between the hydroxyl (O–H) groups of PS-CDs and –NH and –COOH groups of IAA. Electrostatic interactions are observed between the polar C=O and C–O groups, while π–π stacking interactions likely occur between the aromatic rings of IAA and conjugated systems present on the PS-CDs. Additionally, van der Waals forces may stabilize the complex through interactions between the alkyl chains of IAA and hydrophobic regions on the PS-CDs. These interactions are confirmed by shifts and changes in the FT-IR spectra, which in turn results in aggregation of PS-CDs. Also, after the addition of IAA, the zeta potential value decreased to −17.08 mV (Fig. S6d of Supporting Information). This reduction in zeta potential is indicative of electrostatic interactions between the PS-CDs (probe) and IAA (analyte), which likely neutralizes some of the surface charges on the PS-CDs, reducing the repulsion between them and facilitating to from PS-CDs-IAA nanoaggregation. This aggregation decreases the effective concentration of free PS-CDs and promotes the formation of non-fluorescent complexes, thereby enhancing the quenching effect without significantly impacting the fluorescence lifetime. Overall, the quenching mechanism is predominantly static, driven by the formation of ground-state complexes and further enhanced by the aggregation of PS-CDs in the presence of IAA. The schematic representation of the quenching after the addition of IAA is depicted in Fig. S11 of Supporting Information.

To evaluate the selectivity of PS-CDs as a fluorescent probe, a comprehensive investigation was performed to understand how the presence of various potential chemical interfering agents affects its fluorescence emission. The study involved exposing the PS-CDs to a range of biomolecules, including quercetin, mitomycin-C, ATP, L-alanine, and L-threonine, ascorbic acid, stigmasterol, thiamine, beta-carotene, glucose, malic acid each at a concentration of 1000 μM. Additionally, the response of PS-CDs was tested in the presence of several common pesticides—metsulfuron, metalaxyl, Acephate, Carbendazim, and Isoprothiolane also at 1000 μM. Furthermore, the influence of different plant hormones, such as abscisic acid (ABA), gibberellic acid (GA), and naphthalene acetic acid (NAA), each at 1000 μM, was also assessed. The various metal ions such as Mg2+, K+, Zn2+, Fe3+ at 1000 μM concentration was also tested for specificity of the developed probe. The fluorescence emission intensity of PS-CDs was meticulously measured after the introduction of each of these chemical species to determine the specificity of PS-CDs in the presence of potential interfering chemical species. The experimental data demonstrated that the fluorescence emission intensity of PS-CDs remained stable and did not exhibit significant changes upon the addition of any of the tested biomolecules, pesticides, metal ions or hormones (Fig. S13a of Supporting Information). This stability indicates that PS-CDs maintain their fluorescent properties in the presence of these interfering chemical compounds, suggesting minimal or no interaction with these substances under the experimental conditions.

However, a marked deviation was observed when IAA was introduced to PS-CDs. Specifically, the addition of IAA led to a substantial quenching of the fluorescence emission of PS-CDs, as shown in (Fig. S13b of Supporting Information). This significant decrease in the fluorescence intensity upon exposure to IAA indicates a strong and specific interaction between the PS-CDs and IAA, resulting in the quenching effect. The results imply that among the various tested chemical species, PS-CDs possess a distinct and selective recognition capability for IAA, making them a potentially useful probe for the detection of IAA hormone. The ability of PS-CDs to selectively respond to IAA, while remaining unaffected by other biomolecules, pesticides, metal ions and hormones, underscores their potential application in environments where selective hormone detection is crucial.

A cellulose paper strip detection method was developed for instantaneous application in agricultural sectors. The paper strips were fabricated as described in the experimental section. When illuminated under UV light at 365 nm, the strips exhibited green fluorescence. To assess their sensitivity to IAA, the strips were immersed in solutions of varying IAA concentrations, specifically 0.05, 0.5, 5.0, 10, 100, and 500 µM. Following immersion, the cellulose strips were air-dried at room temperature and subsequently observed under UV illumination. The fluorescence intensity of the strips varied noticeably in response to the different IAA concentrations. Notably, as the IAA concentration increased, the fluorescence color transitioned from bright green to light green, as depicted in Fig. 6 a. The graphical representation suggest that after the impregnation of IAA the intensity was decreased. The colour intensity was recorded using ImageJ software, under UV lamp at 365 nm (Fig. 6b). This gradient in fluorescence intensity underscores the strips sensitivity to IAA, enabling the detection of different concentrations of this plant hormone. The visible alteration in fluorescence under UV light confirms the potential of this method for real-time monitoring and detection of IAA in agricultural environments. The ability to visually discern changes in the fluorescence intensity corresponds to varying IAA concentrations, demonstrating the practical utility of PS-CDs modified cellulose strips as a simple and effective tool for on-site analysis of IAA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of IAA in vegetable samples (real-time applications)
ce:section.ce:para: Following the investigation of the qualitative determination of IAA using PS-CDs, a practical study was conducted using carrot, apple, and tomato samples to evaluate the developed probe's real-world applicability. The vegetable samples were prepared following the procedures outlined earlier, which involved extracting the relevant components and filtering the resultant solution through Whatman filter paper. The filtrate obtained was then mixed with the PS-CDs probe. To further assess the probe's performance, the PS-CDs were combined with vegetable samples that had been spiked with varying concentrations of IAA. The spectral analysis revealed the significant changes in the fluorescence properties of the PS-CDs upon the addition of the spiked solutions, indicating the prepared PS-CDs showed effective response to IAA in these complex matrices. The recovery rates of IAA from the spiked vegetable samples were found to be within the range of 96.05–99.98 % with relative standard deviations (RSD) ranging from 0.30 % to 1.74 %, as detailed in Table S1. These results demonstrate that the PS-CDs probe is capable of detecting IAA in carrot, apple, and tomato samples with high reliability and accuracy. The narrow range of RSD values (<2) further underscores the consistency and precision of the developed probe in real-world applications, confirming its potential for effective use in agricultural and food safety contexts. To evaluate practicability and reliability of the probe the intra-day and inter-day analysis was performed. As presented in Table S2 of Supporting Information, the method showed reliable precision (RSD values under 2 %), and accuracy ranging from 0.08 to 1.34 % for the analysis of IAA in carrot, apple and tomato samples. The results of the developed method were validated by comparison with an established HPLC method. To measure IAA in carrot, tomato, and apple samples, 0.25 μM of IAA was spiked into each sample. The concentration of IAA was then determined using HPLC by creating a calibration graph. As shown in Table S3 of Supporting Information, the HPLC method demonstrated recovery rates of 96.22 to 97.14 % with a relative standard deviation (RSD) below 2 %. These findings indicate that the recoveries obtained with the PS-CDs-based fluorescence method align closely with those from the HPLC method. This suggests that PS-CDs could be employed as a reliable, straightforward probe for the fluorescence detection of IAA in vegetables and fruits, offering an alternative portable analytical approach for IAA assay in real samples.

In this study, photoluminescent PS-CDs were successfully synthesized from Plectranthus scutellarioides leaves extract and used as a fluorescence probe for the detection of IAA. The as-synthesized PS-CDs exhibited excellent photophysical properties, including a broad absorbance at 330 nm and a strong green fluorescence emission at 530 nm when excited at 450 nm. The PS-CDs demonstrated a high QY of 21.72 %, indicating efficient fluorescence, which is vital for their application as a sensing probe. The probe was highly selective for IAA, showing significant fluorescence quenching in its presence, whereas other chemical interfering agents are remained unaffected the fluorescence properties of PS-CDs. The fluorescence quenching mechanism was attributed to static quenching, likely driven by the formation of ground-state complexes between the PS-CDs and IAA, facilitated by hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions, and π-π stacking. The study further validated the practical application of the PS-CDs in real-time IAA detection, including the development of a simple and effective cellulose strip-based sensor capable of visually detecting varying concentrations of IAA. Additionally, the probe was successfully applied to the detection of IAA in complex vegetable matrices, demonstrating high recovery rates and precision. Overall, the PS-CDs synthesized in this work present a robust and efficient platform for the selective and sensitive detection of IAA, with potential applications in agricultural monitoring, food safety, and environmental analysis.
